data.title: New Opportunities for Domestic Stablecoins Under Regulation
data.description:
Author: Prathik Desai Source: tokendispatch Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Funds only truly function when they reach their destination. Salaries earned overseas can only be used to pay for rent, tuition, utilities, and groceries in the home country after passing through banks, forex brokers, payment partners, and local compliance audits. Before that, it is merely value in motion, not a medium of exchange.

The same issue now arises on-chain. Stablecoins facilitate global fund flows through code, but their practicality depends on the access scenarios, usage permissions, and reserve and redemption rules.

This viewpoint impressed me deeply when I studied Dune's report, "Beyond De-dollarization: The Rise of Native Currency Stablecoins."

In this quantitative analysis, I will analyze the core factors influencing the development of non-dollar, native currency-pegged stablecoins.

The Teeth of Regulation: Nothing illustrates the crucial role of regulation better than the fate of Tether's Euro stablecoin (EURT). The EU's Crypto-Asset Markets Regulation (MiCA) came into effect in 2024, effectively tolling the death knell for Euro Tether. As one of the earliest and once largest non-USD stablecoins, EURT's circulating supply plummeted from over $400 million to approximately $50 million. Consequently, the total circulating supply of global local currency stablecoins fell from $1 billion to $350 million. Crypto enthusiasts often believe that code alone is enough. They issue tokens, inject liquidity, and expect the market to function on its own. However, non-USD stablecoins are not abstract internet currencies; they attempt to become superior digital versions of local currencies like the Euro, Yen, and Thai Baht, circulating on public blockchains and unrestricted by bank operating hours. Yet, they must operate within the financial systems of their respective countries, adhering to reserve requirements, licensing regulations, payment network rules, and redemption expectations. The exit of EURT serves as a warning: first-mover advantage and scale are insufficient. Changes in local regulatory rules can easily erase first-mover advantage. However, regulation is not entirely detrimental to stablecoins. If that were the case, the non-USD stablecoin market should have stagnated after the EURT shutdown. Excluding EURT, the total supply of non-USD stablecoins nearly tripled, from approximately $350 million in January 2023 to $1.1 billion in February 2026. Expanding the Market: During the same period, the number of addresses holding such stablecoins increased from approximately 42,000 to over 1.2 million. Monthly transaction volume rose from $600 million to $10 billion, a 16-fold increase; the number of monthly transaction addresses increased 22-fold, from approximately 6,000 to 135,000. The growth rates of both holders and transactors exceeded the supply growth rate, indicating that the market continued to expand due to increased participation. This shows that regulation doesn't always disrupt the market like it did with EURT; instead, it attracts more stablecoin issuers and users. Where are non-USD funds flowing? As of early 2026, unclassified transfer transactions accounted for 38% of the total transfer volume of domestic currency stablecoins. This portion likely corresponds to payment and settlement activities, including peer-to-peer transfers and transfers from self-custodied wallets to payment service providers. Secondly, lending accounts for 29%, decentralized exchange (DEX) trading accounts for 17%, and centralized exchange (CEX) related fund flows account for 14%. This structure indicates that non-USD stablecoins have two main on-chain uses: payments and fund transfers between individuals or businesses. They are also used in basic DeFi scenarios such as lending and trading. However, this data rests on a crucial premise: if euro-pegged stablecoins are excluded, the market trend will be drastically different. Euro stablecoins account for over 90% of total transactions, and their usage is closer to that of independent financial assets. Users deposit them into lending markets, use them for trading on decentralized exchanges, and view them primarily as on-chain cash that can generate interest, be used as collateral, and circulate within DeFi, making native currency stablecoins appear more mature. EuroC, along with euro stablecoins such as EURS, EURM, and EUROe, have been integrated into DeFi interest-earning protocols like Aave, Morpho, and Fluid. If euro stablecoins are excluded, the remaining non-USD digital currencies are primarily used for settlement infrastructure. Nearly 80% of non-USD and non-EUR stablecoins flow to unclassified transfers, mainly including wallet fund transfers, corporate debt settlements, quasi-cross-border remittances, and payment flows completed through service providers. The dominant position of EUR stablecoins among non-USD stablecoins indicates that the next phase of growth is more likely to focus on DeFi infrastructure scenarios. Non-USD stablecoins other than EUR will first achieve scale as localized financial infrastructure on the digital track before entering DeFi application scenarios. This growth is crucial because it will cover scenarios such as payroll, fund management, merchant settlement, cross-border remittances, and foreign exchange. These areas are far more regulated than basic DeFi scenarios because operational funds cannot tolerate the ambiguity of regulations as they do with speculative assets. For tokens to operate within local payment systems, fund management processes, and highly compliant environments, they must have predictable reserves, clear redemption procedures, and a defined legal status. Therefore, regulation will play a key role in the adoption of non-USD stablecoins. This also explains why growth is occurring in regions with mature financial systems. The report points out that stablecoin activity related to the Brazilian Real (BRL) and Japanese Yen (JPY) has accelerated significantly after the establishment of local regulatory frameworks; while markets lacking specific regulatory systems, such as Indonesia, have lagged behind. I also believe that non-USD stablecoins have economic value. Cross-border payments still face high exchange costs, and cross-border remittances suffer significant losses due to exchange rate differences and intermediary losses. More local currency stablecoins can reduce the need for funds to transit through the US dollar before reaching their destination, lowering exchange costs, eliminating settlement frictions, and allowing businesses and individuals to hold and use the currencies used for their income, expenditures, and savings. Its potential extends far beyond the DeFi sector. Euro stablecoins have already set a benchmark for the integration of local digital currencies into the financial system. From a global perspective, reducing the cost of cross-border capital flows, improving efficiency, and reducing dependence on the US dollar will bring far-reaching value. Issuers that enable easier transfer and settlement of local currencies and embed them into existing payment infrastructure will benefit from the enormous potential of non-US dollar stablecoins. If an environment conducive to widespread adoption can be created, DeFi integration will naturally follow.

data.title: Vietnam Arrests Suspects Over ONUS Crypto Scam Using Fake Tokens to Defraud Millions
data.description:
Vietnamese Authorities Dismantle Multi-Billion-Dollar Crypto Scam Targeting Millions

Millions of Vietnamese investors were reportedly defrauded through a large-scale cryptocurrency scheme centred on the ONUS platform, police said, as authorities arrested key figures behind the operation.

🚨SCAM: VIETNAM AUTHORITIES LAUNCH PROBE INTO MULTI-BILLION DOLLAR CRYPTO FRAUD

One of Vietnam's largest cryptocurrency fraud cases to date has transpired, with authorities detaining several high-profile figures accused of a multiyear scheme to misappropriate assets.… pic.twitter.com/cOySfZYCkm

— BSCN (@BSCNews) March 27, 2026

Who Led The ONUS Fraud Network

Police identified Vuong Le Vinh Nhan, a fintech and blockchain entrepreneur also known as Eric Vuong, along with six accomplices, as central to the scam.

⭐Chân dung người đứng sau sàn tiền số ONUS

- ONUS được sáng lập năm 2020 bởi ông Trần Quang Chiến cùng đội ngũ người Việt, hướng đến cộng đồng người dùng trong nước và quốc tế.

- Sàn ONUS được phát triển từ nền tảng VNDC, đến năm 2025, nền tảng này… https://t.co/X2nzDB9PkEpic.twitter.com/pAFqeJFNLZ

— DAVID🔶BNB (@davidbnb68) March 22, 2026

Investigators allege the group promoted and traded fake digital tokens, including VNDC, ONUS, and HNG, manipulating supply, demand, and prices while presenting them as legitimate investment opportunities.

Tran Quang Chien, the technical administrator of the ONUS exchange, and Ngo Thi Thao, director of HanaGold Jewelry JSC, were also among those detained.

this scam demon (Tran Quang Chien) controlled the PROFIT/LOSS of investors on ONUS exchange

Now he was arrested by VN polices

The case remains very complex,the Vietnamese Ministry of Public Security is expanding its investigation into money laundering by the HVA ONUS Hanagold pic.twitter.com/okE75stncn

— Bee Network - Vietnam 🇻🇳 (@BeeNetwork_VN) March 28, 2026

Ngo Thi Thao, Tran Quang Chien, and Vuong Le Vinh Nhan

How The Scheme Operated Across Cities

The Ministry of Public Security said the investigation spanned multiple cities, including Hanoi, Ho Chi Minh City, Da Nang, Can Tho, and Dak Lak.

Over 140 individuals were summoned for questioning, and authorities seized computers, mobile devices, documents, and other evidence to prevent the suspects from destroying data or dispersing assets.

🇻🇳 VIETNAM BUSTS $BILLION CRYPTO PONZI

ONUS platform:
• 7 arrested, 140+ questioned
• VNDC, ONUS, HNG tokens under central control
• Price manipulation via wash trading
• "Billions raised" — but market cap 100x smaller
• March 20: platform simply vanished

The scheme:
1.…

— VisionSEC (@visions3c) March 28, 2026

Criminal proceedings were launched on 23 March 2026 for “using computer networks, telecommunications, or electronic means to commit asset misappropriation” and “money laundering,” covering activities from 2018 to the present.

How Investors Were Misled

Authorities allege the group used false promotions and coordinated trading to inflate token prices artificially, keeping central control over the markets.

Investors reportedly lost billions of dollars, although police have not provided a detailed breakdown.

One victim said they were “devastated” after losing over US$15,000.

The ONUS platform, formerly known as VNDC Wallet, had marketed itself as a digital asset ecosystem offering trading, staking, and investment products with more than seven million users globally by the end of 2025.

What The Corporate Links Reveal

ONUS is linked to Singapore-based XPLOR and backed by the US fintech company Vemanti Group.

Vemanti confirmed it learned of the indictments through the ministry and local media and engaged US legal counsel to assess the situation.

Nhan Vuong was identified as the company’s board chairman and Chien Tran as a board member.

Despite ONUS claiming over seven million users, market data aggregator CoinMarketCap lists its token with a self-reported market capitalisation of around US$25 million, highlighting a discrepancy between reported user activity and the value of assets.

Why Vietnam Is a Hotspot For Crypto Scams

Vietnam has allowed blockchain technology to grow in a legal grey area, banning crypto for payments but permitting speculation.

The country has emerged as one of the most active retail digital asset markets, with an estimated 17 million people owning cryptocurrencies, according to a 2025 Chainalysis ranking.

With over 17 million people owning crypto assets in 2024, Vietnam is positioning itself as one of the pioneering countries in blockchain. pic.twitter.com/mn9zUJGgaE

— Bobby 🧋 (@Frenzland_) February 10, 2025

Police urged victims to come forward with more information as investigations continue into the large-scale fraud network.

data.title: In the AI ​​era, rewards will only be given to those with the knowledge.
data.description:
On Sunday morning, while having coffee at the Palo Alto zombie cafe, Alan Walker looked out the window and casually remarked:

Many people still think that the AI ​​era is about diligence, execution, overtime, and manpower.

Actually, it's not.

The cruelest thing about the AI ​​era is that rewards won't be distributed equally. They will only go to those who truly have the knowledge. This applies to product development, technology, and investment.

AIIt's not a tool upgrade, it's the arrival of high intelligence

Many people today still understand AI using internet thinking, which is already too late.

The internet was a revolution in information distribution; AI is a revolution in intelligent distribution.

The internet was a revolution in information distribution; AI is a revolution in intelligent distribution.

Previously, you used tools, but the tools didn't understand you; now you're facing a highly intelligent agent that can understand, deduce, collaborate, and even amplify your insights. What does this mean? It means that AI is no longer just saving you time, but is beginning to directly participate in judgment, structure generation, reducing trial-and-error costs, and even redefining "how much a person can actually do." AI is not an enhanced version of old tools; it represents the first large-scale entry of high intelligence into the workflow of ordinary people. Therefore, in this era, the first to be rewarded are not the busiest people, but those who first realize that what they are facing is no longer software, but a new intelligent infrastructure. AI only amplifies cognition, it doesn't improve one's brain. Many people believe that using AI automatically upgrades them. Actually, it's the opposite. AI is inherently very cruel. It doesn't improve everyone equally; it only amplifies each person's existing structure, judgment, and boundaries. If you lack a framework in your mind, AI will often just give you more elegant nonsense. If you lack the ability to abstract problems, it can only keep you on the surface. Only when you have a clear understanding of goals, constraints, costs, and paths will it truly unleash its potential, giving you deeper reasoning and stronger output. AI doesn't give a cheat code to a useless brain; it simply amplifies the human mind. So why do some people become stronger with the use of AI, while others become more mediocre? It's not that the model is biased; it's that the cognitive levels are too far apart. The rewards of technology flow first to those who understand the direction. This has always been the case in the tech industry: the real big rewards are never given to those who "work the hardest," but to those who "understand the next paradigm." The steam engine era rewarded those who understood mechanization; the internet era rewarded those who understood connection and distribution; and the mobile internet era rewarded those who understood entry points and network effects. In the AI ​​era, rewards are beginning to flow to those who understand "how high intelligence rewrites production relations." Many programmers today are still struggling with how to use AI as a plugin, an assistant, or an efficiency-enhancing tool. But more insightful people are already thinking on another level: Which jobs will this high intelligence swallow up, which processes will be rewritten, which organizations will be restructured, which product boundaries will be changed, and ultimately, to whom will value be redistributed? The greatest benefits of technology have never been given to those who know how to use the tools, but to those who first understand what the tools will destroy and what they will rebuild. This is the difference. While ordinary people are still learning how to use them, those with a higher level of understanding are already redrawing the map. The rewards of investment are only given to those who understand the core of the times. Investment is actually more ruthless. Because when making products, mistakes can be corrected gradually; but when investing, a wrong direction can lead to losses in three to five years. The most valuable investors in the AI ​​era are not the best storytellers or the most trend-chasing, but those who understand first: This isn't just an opportunity to "add another track," it's an opportunity to reorder the entire technology value chain. What makes a knowledgeable investor? It's not just about looking at valuations, growth, and hype. It's about understanding: Whose profits will be eaten first by high intelligence, whose competitive advantage will be compressed first, whose value will be repriced first, and where profits and power will ultimately be concentrated. Therefore, discerning investors don't see "whether a certain AI application is popular or not"; they see: which layer in this entire chain—model, computing power, data, interfaces, workflow, organizational efficiency, and user relationships—is most likely to generate long-term returns, which layer is merely a toll, and which layer will ultimately be consumed by the native model. The greatest rewards in investing don't go to the most enthusiastic participants, but to those who understand the endgame earliest. This is why many people appear to be investing in AI, yet their final returns can be vastly different. Because some are chasing volatility, while others are betting on structure. For the first time, humanity is being forced to revalue itself by highly intelligent technology. The deepest layer of this issue isn't technology or investment, but rather humanity itself. The machines humans have encountered in the past were low-intelligence machines. They did the work, did the calculations, and connected to the internet, but they didn't force us to confront ourselves. Today's AI is different. For the first time, it forces many to admit that many of their previously prized abilities were merely localized advantages from an older era. Therefore, many people's true emotion towards AI isn't excitement, but discomfort. Because once you truly delve into AI, you'll discover that: Many skills that were once valuable are rapidly depreciating; many skills that were once considered unattainable are being diluted by the system; and the difference between many individuals is no longer reflected in the level of effort, but in the depth of their understanding. The most damaging aspect of AI isn't that it's faster than you, but that it makes you realize you're not as valuable as you thought. This is why most people talk about AI but dare not truly engage with it. It's not because the barrier to entry is high, but because the barrier of self-esteem is high.

In this era, rewards will not be given to people of the old era

I remember Alan's last sentence very clearly. He said: "This era doesn't deny people opportunities; it doesn't pay old mindsets with old-fashioned money. If you treat AI as a toy, it will play with you; if you treat AI as a search engine, it will search with you; only when you treat AI as a highly intelligent collaborator will it elevate you. The same applies to investment and technology. If you don't understand the rule changes brought about by high intelligence, even if you're riding the wave, you won't achieve truly significant results." Therefore, what this article truly wants to say is not "those with knowledge are more powerful," but rather: In the AI ​​era, rewards won't be equally distributed to everyone. They will only be given to those who first understand high intelligence, first comprehend the new rules, and first dare to reinvent themselves. This applies to people in the tech industry, investors, and ordinary people alike. AI doesn't reward every participant; it only realizes the insights of a few, producing results that the majority can never achieve.

data.title: Harness, JJ Lin, the trillion-dollar racetrack, and the palm of Anthrapic
data.description:
In the Agent era, the opportunities and imagination in the Infra sector far exceed those of a "lobster." In March 2026, the hottest word in the AI ​​industry wasn't the name of any model, but an English word that seemed completely unrelated to AI: Harness. Its original meaning is horse harness—reins, bridles, saddle, and all the equipment worn on a horse. As a verb, it means "to control." You wouldn't say "harness a calculator," but you would say "harness the wind" or "harness a horse." Whether intentionally or unintentionally, those who use this term are acknowledging one thing: they are not facing a passive tool, but a powerful and autonomous entity. They are not "using" it, but "controlling" it. This term is becoming the core industry concept of the AI ​​Agent era. Around it, a trillion-dollar infrastructure layer is growing. And the rule-makers for this infrastructure layer are emerging. Harness is the New Infrastructure. Harness has two evolutionary threads in the AI ​​context. The first is the rhetorical layer. "Harness AI" has long been a generic term in the tech industry, essentially meaning "the ability to harness AI." The second point is more important and pertains to the technical level. In late 2025, Anthropic began using "harness" to describe the infrastructure built around its AI Agent—context management, tool invocation, memory, guardrails, and orchestration. The official definition of the Claude Agent SDK is "a general-purpose agent harness." In early 2026, HashiCorp co-founder Mitchell Hashimoto formally proposed "AI Harness," and "Harness Engineering" rapidly spread as a new field of engineering practice. But what truly makes this term worthy of serious attention is not its popularity, but its precise description of a new relationship emerging between humans and AI: symbiotic asymmetric collaboration. Humans provide intention, judgment, and direction. AI provides capability, speed, and scale. Harness acknowledges both the asymmetry of capability and the asymmetry of authority, and these two asymmetries are reversed—AI's capabilities may far exceed those of its driver, but the driver retains the ultimate power to determine direction. A horse runs much faster and is much stronger than a human, but where it goes is determined by the human. Humans need to harness AI that is more powerful than themselves. This is perhaps the most accurate expression of Anthropic's choice of this word—whether intentional or unintentional. And the word Harness does indeed sound quite "Anthropic," implying a human-centered approach. Some say: Harness is the New Datasets. This statement is intuitive, but the conclusion is inaccurate. When base models converge, the quality of Harness does indeed become a key variable determining the quality of the Agent, just as data quality can determine the life or death of a base model. However, Datasets and Harness exist in fundamentally different ways: Datasets occupy a single position in the technical architecture, namely the input during the training phase; but Harness is not a specific layer, but a stack, a combination of layers. Context engineering and memory are the storage layer, tool access is the network layer, orchestration is the container layer, guardrails are the security layer, evaluation is the observability layer, and skill encapsulation is middleware. Each layer can grow into independent companies, standards, and business models. This is completely isomorphic to the stack structure of cloud computing infrastructure. In this sense, Harness is the New Infra. It's not the infra for model pre-training, but rather the infra for building agents, giving them autonomy while ensuring they strictly follow human instructions, guarantee safety, and adhere to rules. Harness itself isn't a new dataset, but a well-functioning Harness generates good datasets for agents and establishes a data flywheel. When a harness accumulates enough user behavior data and domain knowledge, it's no longer just an add-on system design; it begins to possess the attributes of data: it gets better with use and becomes increasingly difficult to replace. From this, we can derive an equation that can almost be used as a definition: Base Model + Harness = Agent. The base model provides the basic capabilities—reasoning, generation, and understanding. But it is static, passive, and directionless. It can do everything, so it is nothing. Harness provides structure, direction, and constraints, converging infinite possibilities into finite, purposeful actions. The moment the two combine, the AI ​​transforms from an object being questioned into an agent that acts. The same horse, fitted with different harnesses, can pull a cart, carry people, plow fields, and race. The design of Harness determines the form and purpose of the Agent. On March 26, 2026, Junyang Lin, the former technical lead of the Qwen team, published a long article on X titled "From 'Reasoning' Thinking to 'Agentic' Thinking." Within two days, it garnered 700,000 views, 2,800 likes, and 677 shares. Three weeks earlier, on March 4, he had just left Alibaba. Three weeks later, he wrote a systematic industry analysis article. The core argument of the article is that AI is shifting from "thinking longer" to "thinking for action." Reasoning Thinking is essentially a static monologue—the model generates increasingly longer chains of reasoning in a closed space, attempting to compensate for the lack of interaction with the environment with more text. Agentic Thinking, on the other hand, continuously advances the task through interaction with the environment. The training process involved a three-stage leap: from training the model, to training the agent, to training the system. This isn't just empty talk. He uses Qwen's own practical experience to illustrate this: merging the thinking and instruct patterns is far more difficult than imagined. The data distribution and optimization goals of the two behaviors are fundamentally at odds—instruct prioritizes simplicity, speed, and format compliance, while thinking seeks to spend more tokens exploring alternative paths. After attempting to merge them, Qwen3 reverted to independent lines. This lesson points to a deeper insight: Instruct is a replacement for Harness in the pre-Agent era. Instruct, through SFT and RLHF, "burns" behavioral norms into the model weights—essentially sewing the reins into the horse's muscles. This was sufficient in the question-and-answer era. However, in the Agent era, models must operate autonomously, invoke tools, and make continuous decisions; the behavioral space expands explosively, making it impossible to train all constraints into the weights. The focus of control must shift from inside the model to outside. The capabilities of Instruct have been breached by the Agent paradigm, making Harness an inevitable evolution. Lin Junyang mentions "harness" four times in his article, with a very clear progression: from "the external environment in which the agent runs," to "an independent engineering practice—harness engineering," and then to "part of the training object—the agent and the harness around it." His article proves one thing from the training side: Harness is not only the infrastructure for agent runtime, but also the infrastructure for agent training. In the closed loop of Agentic RL, the agent runs in Harness, the environment generates feedback signals, the feedback drives RL to update the policy, and the policy changes the agent's behavior. Removing Harness doesn't just slow down the agent; it makes training impossible. Furthermore, he explicitly stated that the biggest bottleneck in Agentic RL is not the algorithm or the model architecture, but rather the environment quality and rollout infrastructure. The bottleneck restricting Agent evolution lies in the Infra layer. Thanks to Junyang for filling in the missing half of my argument that "Harness is the New Infra." Harness is an indispensable infrastructure for Agent runtime, as stated earlier. Junyang's article tells us that Harness is also the Infra during Agent training. In the closed loop of Agentic RL, the environment generates feedback signals, feedback drives policy updates, the policy changes Agent behavior, and Agent behavior triggers new environmental feedback. A system layer that is indispensable on both the training and inference ends is the true infrastructure, or Harness. Lin Junyang made a profound statement in his article: "Environment building is transforming from a side project into a genuine entrepreneurial category." "Environment building" is not the same as Harness, but rather a subset of Harness, yet a crucial one. "Environment" primarily corresponds to tool access and evaluation feedback in the Harness architecture—specifically, the world that the Agent interacts with during training: the code execution sandbox, browser simulator, test case set, and API simulation layer. Its core function is to generate feedback signals, giving Agentic RL something to optimize. It's somewhat like a container for Agent training and setup, a benchmark, and a Hugging Face. The environment is the training ground for the Agent, and Harness is the complete set of equipment for the Agent to run. The training ground is part of the equipment, but not the whole thing. However, when a former technical lead of an open-source model starts defining a separate startup category for a submodule of Harness, this in itself is a signal—it indicates that this stack has become complex and valuable enough, and is beginning to grow into independent business entities layered like a real infrastructure stack. And a startup track is defined in a long, academically-oriented article. If you don't think this counts as Lin Junyang's startup pitch deck, then you shouldn't be a VC.

A Trillion-Dollar Startup Track

If Lin Junyang really goes ahead and develops Agent training environment infrastructure—the direction he himself defined as "the real startup category"—which layer of the Harness pie is he facing? And how big is this layer of the pie?

Harness is internally a complete multi-layered architecture, which can be broken down into seven core modules: Context Engineering, Memory System, Tool Access, Skill Encapsulation, Guardrails and Permissions, Evaluation and Feedback, and Orchestration and State Management.

Except for the Tool Access Layer (MCP), there are startups running in each layer.

The context and memory layer has Cognee (€7.5 million funding) and Interloom ($16.5 million seed round, with Sequoia participating). The tool access layer is standardized by the MCP protocol—97 million monthly SDK downloads; Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and Amazon have all adopted it, with few startups. The security access layer has seen the emergence of Runlayer ($11 million, led by Khosla), while guardrails and compliance have Guardrails AI, Vigilant AI, Runtime, and Alter. Evaluation and observability are the hottest areas, with Arize AI raising $70 million in a Series C round; clients include Uber and PepsiCo, and Langfuse has become an open-source community standard. The orchestration layer presents a "three-way competition" pattern: LangGraph, CrewAI ($18 million in funding, used by 60% of Fortune 500 companies), and Microsoft Agent Framework, two of which are startups. The Skills encapsulation layer is dominated by startups focusing on Agent products in vertical industries. Its benchmarks are Harvey—legal AI, valued at $11 billion, with $1 billion in cumulative funding and an ARR of $190 million, and Abridge—healthcare AI, valued at $5.3 billion. The training environment layer is in its earliest stage, with approximately 20 seed-stage companies. Wing VC predicts this will consolidate to 3-5 by 2030. However, not every module represents a promising sector. The core criterion for judging whether a track is still bad is: does this module solve a "model capability problem" or a "system design problem"? The former will be swallowed up by the base model—the context window expands from 128K to 1M and then even larger; today's sophisticated compression strategy may be useless tomorrow. Modules at the system design layer, on the other hand, have lasting value—for example, tool integration is a niche issue; safety barriers are compliance issues; evaluation is an independence issue, and these cannot be resolved simply by making the model stronger. Their exit paths are also drastically different. Tool integration and skill encapsulation are too close to the model, giving model manufacturers a strong incentive to acquire them—Anthropic's MCP and Skills, and OpenAI's Plugins and GPTs, are both about acquiring these two layers. Starting a business in these two directions has a ceiling of being acquired. Guardrail compliance and assessment observability are the opposite; they naturally require third-party independence. Banks won't trust Anthropic's own compliance audit tools, just as you wouldn't let the auditee issue their own audit report. Independence isn't a business strategy; it's the product's inherent value. The former are good acquisition targets, the latter are good IPO targets. They all belong to Harness, the infrastructure of agents. So how big is the overall Harness sector? From bottom to top, summing up the valuation space of the seven sub-tracks, the total valuation of independent startups by 2030 is estimated at approximately $500-800 billion. Skill encapsulation and vertical knowledge have the largest market share ($250-350 billion), while guardrails and compliance are growing the fastest (CAGR 65.8%, from $700 million in 2024 to a projected $109.9 billion in 2034; the more autonomous the agent, the more expensive the reins). Training environments are in the earliest stage but have the highest certainty. The overall AI Agent market is projected to generate $50-100 billion in revenue by 2030. Harness, as an Infra layer, will account for approximately 40-50%. Based on a 10-15x PS multiple for SaaS/Infra, the valuation space is reasonably consistent. The startup landscape is worth nearly a trillion dollars. If we include the revenue from Harness embedded in the model factory, the overall valuation of the Harness infrastructure layer is between $2.5 and $3.8 trillion. This is roughly equivalent to the total market capitalization of the entire cloud computing infrastructure layer today. Returning to Lin Junyang, if he truly enters the Harness sub-sector of training environments and RL infrastructure, he faces a market with only about 20 seed-stage companies currently, but a valuation potential of $20-50 billion by 2030. Wing VC predicts that this sector will eventually consolidate into 3-5 leading companies. Given his background as the former CTO of Qwen, in Silicon Valley, his seed round valuation would likely be between $200 million and $500 million. The market doesn't value the company; it values ​​the person. Lin Junyang no longer needs to write a business plan; that tweet suffices. If he were to raise a dollar fund in China, his valuation would start at $50 million, and $100 million is not out of the question. As for RMB? We'll see. Now, a truly important question needs to be answered: Who defines the rules for Harness, this trillion-dollar infrastructure layer? Let's look at the following harsh facts: MCP is the standard protocol promoted by Anthropic. Claude Code is a harness product created by Anthropic, generating $2.5 billion in annual revenue. The Agent SDK is a developer portal built by Anthropic. The Skills system was designed by Anthropic. Even the popularity of the word "harness" in the context of AI agents is largely driven by Anthropic. A deeper reason lies in the business model. OpenAI's core narrative is the "strongest model," with revenue primarily coming from ChatGPT subscriptions. Anthropic completely abandons multimodal and world models, yet it is increasingly considered the strongest model. Claude's selling point isn't its top benchmark scores, but rather that it's "the model best suited for agent workflows"—more reliable, more controllable, and better suited for long-term autonomous operation. This positioning means Anthropic's competitiveness comes not only from the model itself, but also from the quality of the Harness ecosystem surrounding it. The improvement of each layer of Harness widens its competitive moat. The prosperity of the Harness ecosystem directly equates to Anthropic's commercial interests. This explains why OpenAI began trying to build its ecosystem as early as 2023—Plugins, GPTs, GPT Store—but none of these ventures succeeded, while Anthropic's MCP, launched at the end of 2024—a year and a half later—became the de facto standard. The fundamental reason behind this is that OpenAI is building an application ecosystem, while Anthropic is building an infrastructure ecosystem. OpenAI's GPT Store operates on the logic of an app store—"I have the largest user base, so you can open a store here." But when the model itself can do everything, applications become unnecessary. GPTs lack differentiation barriers because the underlying capabilities and ChatGPT itself are the same thing. Anthropic's MCP, however, is not an app store, but a protocol store. It doesn't invite developers to open stores on Claude; instead, it defines a set of connectivity standards that all tools and all models can use. This is HTTP logic, not App Store logic. The more open the protocol, the stronger the control within the ecosystem. Currently, everyone uses MCP, which was designed by Anthropic. It doesn't need to lock in users; it locks in the minds and toolchains of developers. At the capital level, Anthropic and early investor Menlo Ventures established the $100 million Anthology Fund, investing in over 30 harness-focused startups in a year. The structure is clever: Menlo provides the capital, Anthropic doesn't participate in the fund's economic interests, but gives each invested company $25,000 in model credits and allows Chief Product Officer Mike Krieger and President Daniela Amodei to participate in demo days. Anthropic secured over 30 startups into the Claude ecosystem without investing a single penny, while simultaneously acquiring cutting-edge demand signals. This is a cost-free option. However, have we considered why, in the era of Agentic AI, Anthropic's protocol ecosystem is more important than OpenAI's application ecosystem? Because Agents are not "applications" in the traditional sense. Traditional app interfaces are fixed and finite—when a user hails a ride, the app calls APIs, matches drivers, and calculates routes according to a preset process. Agents are different; they decide which tools to call, in what order, and when. Their interfaces are infinite and dynamic. Furthermore, Agents need to collaborate—the orchestrator schedules specialized Agents, and specialized Agents schedule sub-Agents; this is a distributed system coordination problem. When the interaction interface is fixed, you can connect to them one by one; when the interaction interface is infinite, you can only define standards. TCP/IP allows any two computers to communicate, HTTP allows any client to access any server, and MCP allows any agent to call any tool. The basic unit of the application ecosystem is the "product," and the basic unit of the protocol ecosystem is the "connection." In the Agentic era, the quantity and quality of connections determine everything. All Harness entrepreneurs are doing somersaults. If Lin Junyang really goes to work on training environment infrastructure, the direction he personally defined in his article as a "real startup category," his product will most likely eventually be integrated into the Claude ecosystem; or establish a parallel ecosystem in China. Because Anthropic defined the protocol, built the SDK, established an ecosystem fund, and captured the minds of developers. It's possible that only Chinese agent entrepreneurs can escape Anthropic's grasp; this is an unavoidable force majeure.

data.title: Polymarket rules have changed. How should those who like to "airdrop" (spread their money) respond?
data.description:
Author: Chloe, ChainCatcher

Polymarket officially announced its updated Market Integrity Rules on March 23, which apply simultaneously to its DeFi platform and US exchanges regulated by the CFTC. The new rules explicitly prohibit three types of insider trading and strengthen the framework for combating market manipulation.

... This policy adjustment did not come out of thin air, but was the product of a series of controversies and public pressure, and also a compliance self-rescue action by Polymarket before being impacted by mainstream US financial regulation. However, the new regulations affect more than just genuine insider players; does it more directly threaten the interests of the vast number of arbitrageurs? Or those professional arbitrageurs who actually provide liquidity? The history of pressure behind the rule upgrade: from the Venezuelan coup to the Iran war. Looking back at the public opinion and regulatory pressure Polymarket has endured in the past few months, in early January 2026, an anonymous user placed a bet of $32,537 on Polymarket, wagering that "Maduro will step down before January 31st." Following Trump's announcement of Maduro's arrest on Truth Social at 4:21 AM, the user immediately received a return of up to $436,000, a return on investment of over 13 times. The investigation revealed that the account was only created in December 2025, and all bets were precisely on the Venezuelan political situation, placed just hours before the events unfolded. Dennis Kelleher, co-founder of Better Markets, pointed out that this transaction exhibited all the characteristics of insider trading: a newly created account, large sums of money, precise timing, and all occurring in an unregulated and opaque market. Coincidentally, around the same time, suspicious trading emerged on Polymarket targeting the "timing of US military action against Iran," with some accounts precisely placing bets on the eve of US military strikes, profiting hundreds of thousands of dollars. It's worth noting that Polymarket CEO Shayne Coplan once made a thought-provoking statement in a CBS News interview: "It's a good thing that insiders have an advantage in the market." However, the reality is that in March 2026, Senators Adam Schiff and John Curtis... A joint bipartisan bill was proposed to ban trading contracts in the prediction market that resemble "sports or casino games." The Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) issued guidelines that same month requiring prediction market platforms to take specific measures to prevent insider trading and encouraging exchanges to proactively consult with regulators to identify "manipulation or price distortion risks" when designing event contracts. This regulatory crackdown is already in place, and Polymarket's policy upgrade is a proactive response to this crackdown. New Rules Analysis: Three Types of Prohibitions and a Multi-Layer Monitoring Framework Polymarket officially released updated market integrity rules on March 23, 2026, clearly defining three red lines: First, transactions based on the theft of confidential information; second, transactions based on illegal sources of information; and third, transactions by those who influence the outcome. At the level of market manipulation, the rules further explicitly prohibit spoofing, wash trading, and fictitious transactions. Regarding these prohibitions, ChainCatcher stated that the boundary between "wash trading" and normal transactions lies in whether real value is generated and whether transaction costs are incurred. Wash trading involves the same group of people transferring funds between themselves, purely for data manipulation; while legitimate arbitrage or market making involves placing limit orders at different price levels and bearing the risk of holding positions. Each transaction is executed with real market users and can withstand scrutiny. In terms of execution architecture, Polymarket employs a "multi-layered monitoring" design. On the DeFi platform side, all transactions are recorded on the Polygon chain, publicly accessible to anyone. The platform collaborates with world-class monitoring technology organizations to detect on-chain anomalies; once suspicious behavior is detected, sanctions can be taken, including blocking wallet addresses and referring users to law enforcement agencies. On the Polymarket US (CFTC-regulated exchange) side, monitoring is divided into three layers: external monitoring technology partners, a real-time monitoring platform, and a regulatory service agreement with the National Futures Association (NFA). The NFA can directly investigate and sanction violators, with sanctions including suspension of qualifications, account termination, monetary fines, or referral to regulatory authorities. What are the interests of arbitrageurs and the predicament of related studios? Polymarket's move is a heavy blow to "insider traders," but it may spark different reactions from arbitrageurs and related studios. The reactions of major market players to the new regulations are intriguing. ChainX, whose Polymarket trading volume has exceeded $200 million, told ChainCatcher that the new regulations were expected, even long anticipated. They believe this is not a blow, but rather a sign of market maturation. Their professional team had already predicted that future market-wide fees and stricter regulations would be introduced when the platform began charging transaction fees. For ordinary airdrop users, the past practice of "wash trading"—relying on creating massive on-chain records and using two accounts to trade in a single market—is now directly impacted by the new regulations. Some players have even evolved to control matrices of 100 wallets or hedge between Polymarket and Kalshi, but the upgraded monitoring system has significantly increased the risks of such behavior. Truly high-quality strategies shouldn't be about "profiteering," but rather genuine arbitrage. Arbitrage itself is a process of identifying price discrepancies and correcting market inefficiencies—a healthy behavior needed for market prediction. As gray-market practices are squeezed out, the market will become cleaner, and professional arbitrageurs may actually see higher returns. The Liquidity Paradox: Are Users Who Boost Volume Parasites or Infrastructure? Furthermore, behind this wave of regulation lies a contradiction that Polymarket cannot avoid: Polymarket's liquidity is not naturally formed. According to on-chain data, 80% of users on the platform wager less than $500 per bet, and the average wager over the past month was only around $100. Therefore, what truly supports market depth are a very small number of large traders and liquidity providers. It is worth exploring whether the group of airdropped farmers who adopt "legitimate strategies" (such as providing two-way liquidity and cross-platform arbitrage) objectively play the role of informal market makers? They narrow the bid-ask spread and enhance market capacity, allowing ordinary users to establish positions at more reasonable prices. On the other hand, from a business perspective, after Polymarket's return to the US market, it urgently needs massive amounts of real trading and depth data to prove the effectiveness of its market to the CFTC (Commodity Futures Trading Commission), which is crucial for obtaining further regulatory approval. If the new regulations are too aggressive and scare away these profit-seeking users, a short-term liquidity crunch is almost inevitable, especially in niche markets where these farmers are often the only source of counterparties. Therefore, the platform should acknowledge the contributions of users who provide genuine liquidity. For example, in a multi-account system, if millions of dollars in trading volume are contributed daily, all of which are maker limit orders, this is precisely what the platform's mechanism encourages. Especially in low-volatility, low-liquidity events, these orders add depth to the order book, allowing ordinary users to execute trades. This behavior essentially exchanges capital and time for rebates, while simultaneously serving the market. Under regulatory compliance, do related studios also need strategic transformation? Polymarket's compliance process can be seen as not a short-term market fluctuation, but a signal of a strategic shift in the platform. From acquiring the licensed exchange QCX to signing an agreement with the NFA, all of this shows that the prediction market is moving closer to traditional financial regulation. On this highly transparent and regulated path, the survival space for traditional "low-quality volume manipulation" will only become narrower. ChainXi believes that the new regulations are actually beneficial to professional teams, and they will take three countermeasures in the future: First, increase liquidity provision to strive for more maker rebates; second, actively discuss deeper market-making solutions with platforms; and third, continuously optimize strategies to increase returns while complying with regulations. Overall, for studios that rely on Polymarket as a core profit source, now is a crucial juncture to shift their strategic focus from "quantity" to "quality." Instead of manipulating 100 wallets for low-quality wash trading and risking precise detection and mass bans by monitoring systems, it's better to abandon the multi-wallet matrix and instead manage a few high-quality accounts. Deep trading through genuine market research or focusing on providing liquidity within the platform's guidelines not only effectively avoids the risk of bans but also increases the likelihood of receiving a more favorable token airdrop allocation due to contributing real value in the final weighted airdrop calculation.

data.title: New Mac Malware Campaign Uses Fake Cloudflare Verification to Target Crypto Holders
data.description:
Mac Users Targeted By Fake Cloudflare CAPTCHAs Leading To Crypto Data Theft

A new malware campaign is exploiting macOS users through counterfeit Cloudflare CAPTCHA pages, putting developers and crypto holders at risk.

Named Infiniti Stealer, this malware quietly siphons cryptocurrency wallet data, browser credentials, and sensitive developer files, challenging the long-held belief that Macs are naturally secure.

How Does The Attack Work

The campaign relies on a social engineering tactic called ClickFix.

Instead of exploiting software vulnerabilities, it tricks users into executing a harmful command themselves.

New #macOS stealer via #ClickFix — First observed using Python + Nuitka onefile — tracking as NukeChain

🍎 Fake Cloudflare CAPTCHA → Terminal paste
🪝 bash dropper — junk-fn pattern shared with MacSync/SHub
🧬 Stage-2: Nuitka arm64 bootstrap (KAY(+zstd)
💻 Stage-3: Python… pic.twitter.com/48dbouIQOz

— Marcelo Rivero (@MarceloRivero) March 24, 2026

Security researchers from Malwarebytes found the attack begins on domains such as update-check[.]com, which closely mimic legitimate Cloudflare human verification pages.

Visitors are prompted to tick a box and then paste a command into Terminal, appearing routine but actually initiating the malware download.

Once the command is executed, the malware silently connects to a remote server controlled by the attacker and begins a three-stage infection.

The initial Bash script decodes and runs the next-stage binary from the /tmp folder, removes macOS quarantine flags, and closes Terminal without alerting the user.

The second stage is an Apple Silicon Mach-O binary built using Nuitka, converting Python into a native C executable that decompresses embedded payloads at runtime, making detection and analysis difficult.

Stage 2 Nuitka Loader Binary running silently on macOS during malware infection. (Source: Malwarebytes)

The final stage, UpdateHelper[.]bin, steals browser credentials, Keychain entries, cryptocurrency wallets, screenshots, and plaintext developer secrets, sending everything to the attacker’s server.

Telegram notifications alert the operator as data is exfiltrated, while captured credentials are queued for password cracking.

Why Macs Are No Longer Immune

ClickFix attacks have traditionally targeted Windows systems, but Infiniti Stealer demonstrates attackers are now adapting this approach for Apple devices.

The malware is compiled into a native macOS binary, avoiding detection from standard antivirus or security software.

Source: X

Analysts note that it also checks for virtualised or sandbox environments and introduces randomised delays to evade automated analysis.

Malwarebytes confirmed Infiniti Stealer as the first documented macOS campaign combining ClickFix delivery with a Nuitka-compiled Python stealer.

Daniel Kelley from SlashNext explained,

“ClickFix is a social engineering attack that tricks users into running malicious commands on their own devices – all under the guise of a routine security check.”

Rising Threats To Personal Crypto Wallets

This campaign reflects a wider trend in crypto-related malware.

A similar macOS threat was also reported in March called GhostClaw, which stole private keys and wallet access via a malicious npm package downloaded by 178 developers before removal.

🚨 GhostClaw Mimic as OpenClaw to Steal Everything from Developers

Source: https://t.co/q9QAi6v1eA

A dangerous malware campaign targeting software developers has surfaced, with a rogue npm package posing as a trusted developer tool to silently drain credentials, crypto… pic.twitter.com/wDIfA3rTD7

— Cyber Security News (@The_Cyber_News) March 10, 2026

Blockchain security firm Chainalysis highlights the growing impact: personal wallet compromises rose from 7.3% of total stolen cryptocurrency in 2022 to 44% in 2024.

Analysts estimate the share could have reached 37% in 2025 without the outsized Bybit exchange attack.

Overall, $3.4 billion was stolen from crypto platforms in 2025.

The cryptocurrency industry witnessed over $3.4B in theft from January through early December 2025, with the February compromise of Bybit alone accounting for $1.5B of that total.

Personal wallet compromises have grown substantially, increasing from just 7.3% of total stolen… pic.twitter.com/tnKCkVlsfb

— Sjuul | AltCryptoGems (@AltCryptoGems) January 7, 2026

Immediate Steps For Users At Risk

Mac users who may have interacted with these fake CAPTCHA pages are advised to stop using the device for sensitive activities, change passwords from a secure system, revoke active sessions, inspect directories such as /tmp and ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ for suspicious files, and run comprehensive malware scans.

No legitimate CAPTCHA page will ever ask a user to open Terminal and paste a command.

Why This Attack Is Hard To Detect

Infiniti Stealer avoids traditional detection by relying entirely on user action.

There is no phishing attachment, malicious download, or software exploit.

It leverages habitual behaviour and “verification fatigue,” convincing users to bypass security instincts.

Analysts warn that this evolution marks a new level of sophistication in social engineering targeting macOS and crypto users.

Users are urged to exercise caution when browsing and never execute commands from untrusted sources, even if the page appears familiar or legitimate.

data.title: CoinShares: The Rack Game Between AI and BTC
data.description:
Source: CoinShares 2026 Q1 Bitcoin Mining Report; Compiled by: Jinse Finance Claw

I. Executive Summary

Q4 2025 was the most challenging quarter for Bitcoin miners since the halving in April 2024. The significant pullback in Bitcoin prices (from an all-time high of approximately $124,500 in early October to approximately $86,000 at the end of December, a drop of about 31%), coupled with near-always high hashrates, led to a hash price compression to a five-year low.

Among publicly traded mining companies, the weighted average cash cost to produce a single Bitcoin rose to approximately $79,995 in Q4 2025.

This quarter saw three defining themes for the industry: Profit Squeeze: Hash prices hit around $36–38/PH/s/day, near or at the break-even point for many miners. Three consecutive downward difficulty adjustments (the first such decline since July 2022) signaled the beginning of miner capitulation. Entering Q1 2026, this figure has further dropped significantly to $29/PH/s/day, suggesting miners will face even more pain. AI/HPC (Artificial Intelligence/High-Performance Computing) Transformation Accelerates: The divide between pure mining companies and new infrastructure companies transforming towards AI is widening further. Currently, publicly traded mining companies have announced AI/HPC contracts totaling over $70 billion. WULF, CORZ, CIFR, and HUT are effectively transforming into data center operators that also engage in Bitcoin mining. Capital Structure Transformation: Several miners have taken on massive debt to finance AI infrastructure construction. IREN currently holds $3.7 billion in convertible notes; WULF has $5.7 billion in total debt; and CIFR has issued $1.7 billion in senior secured notes. The industry's overall leverage has fundamentally altered its risk profile. II. AI and Bitcoin Mining Compete for Rack Space AI continues to compete for rack space in many data centers, which, in the long run, could drive Bitcoin mining towards more intermittent and cheaper energy sources. The migration of Bitcoin miners to AI and high-performance computing is accelerating rapidly. According to recent company announcements, by the end of this year, AI revenue for listed miners could account for as much as 70%, compared to approximately 30% currently. What was initially a strategy for edge diversification is increasingly becoming a core business. During 2025 and early 2026, Bitcoin miners have signed multiple GPU hosting and cloud service agreements with Hyperscalers, totaling over $70 billion. While most of these deals envision building new data centers, they are likely to involve the "cannibalization" or closure of existing mining facilities. Therefore, as the capacity under these contracts is released, the percentage of Bitcoin mining revenue for these operators will significantly decrease throughout 2026. This shift is primarily economically driven. Hash prices are hovering near cyclical lows, compressing mining margins, while AI infrastructure offers more structured and stable returns. In this context, redeploying electricity and capital to HPC seems rational, especially for operators with scalable energy and existing data center capabilities. However, this transformation is not uniform. Some miners, such as IREN and Bitfarms, are actively repositioning themselves as HPC providers, effectively using mining as a bridge to AI infrastructure. Others, such as CleanSpark, continue to prioritize mining in the short term, monetizing recently developed capacity while gradually building AI exposure. A third group remains committed to Bitcoin mining but is evolving its operations. These operators are no longer pursuing hyperscale facilities but are focusing on the lowest-cost and often intermittent energy sources, such as discarded renewable energy or flare gas. For example, Marathon has deployed smaller, localized containerized sites of approximately 10MW at the edge of the energy grid. These configurations are well-suited for mining that can tolerate interruptions but are incompatible with AI workloads that require near-continuous uptime. Load balancing is likely to remain a lasting niche in the mining industry. By providing demand flexibility to grids like ERCOT, miners can obtain more favorable electricity rates. This role is likely to become increasingly important, although it may attract smaller, more specialized operators over time. A core unresolved issue is the sustainability of the AI-driven transformation. While current economic conditions strongly favor AI, mining is highly sensitive to Bitcoin prices. If mining profitability recovers significantly, some operators may reassess their capital allocation between the two activities. In this sense, the current trend may not be a permanent transformation, but rather a functional reflection of relative returns. In the long run, this means the potential emergence of a smaller, pure-play mining group, and a broader group of hybrid infrastructure companies spanning mining and AI. Simultaneously, new entrants may emerge to capitalize on niche markets vacated by existing players, particularly in energy-constrained or highly flexible market segments. The cost difference between Bitcoin mining infrastructure (approximately $700,000-$1,000,000/MW) and AI infrastructure (approximately $8,000,000-$15,000,000/MW) is substantial, and this transition opportunity is currently being realized on a large scale: CORZ: Approximately 350MW of power is used for HPC, and approximately 200MW has begun billing. The contract with CoreWeave has been expanded to 12 years and $10.2 billion. The goal is to reach full capacity of 590MW by early 2027. WULF: 39MW of critical IT capacity is online at Lake Mariner. Contracted HPC revenue totaled $12.8 billion. Additional construction plans continue through Q4 2026. The platform is planned to expand to approximately 2.9GW across five sites. CIFR: Developing a 300MW Barber Lake site in partnership with Fortress Credit Advisors. Reached a multi-billion dollar agreement with Fluidstack (Google-backed). No revenue generated yet. IREN: Expanding to over 10,900 NVIDIA GPUs. The Childress Horizon 1-4 expansion (up to 200MW of liquid-cooled GPUs) is under construction. Q4 AI cloud service revenue reached $17.3 million. HUT: Signed a 15-year, $7 billion lease with Fluidstack for 245MW of capacity at its River Bend, Louisiana campus, with the first data hall targeting early 2027. The failed merger between CORZ and CoreWeave (rejected by shareholders on October 30, 2025) highlights the tension between infrastructure value and equity value. CORZ subsequently restated its financial statements due to improper capitalization of assets to be dismantled during the HPC conversion, reflecting the complexities of accounting treatment. Revenue contributions are still early but growing: CORZ's managed AI/HPC data centers accounted for 39% of fourth-quarter revenue; WULF's HPC accounted for 27%; IREN's AI cloud accounted for 9%; and HIVE's HPC accounted for 5%. Mining remains dominant, but it's clear that AI-driven revenue contributions are growing across almost all companies.

III. Network Hashrate

The Bitcoin network reached a significant milestone at the end of August 2025, breaking the 1 ZH/s (ZetaHash) barrier for the first time. The network hashrate peaked at approximately 1,160 EH/s in early October.

However, a significant decline occurred in the fourth quarter. The hashrate dropped by about 10% from its October high, falling to approximately 1,045 EH/s by the end of December (following a further drop to 850 EH/s in early February before rebounding), and experienced three consecutive negative difficulty adjustments, the first such occurrence since July 2022.

This was primarily driven by the following factors: The Bitcoin price pullback pushed older S19 series hardware below the break-even point (the break-even electricity price for the S19 XP decreased from approximately $0.12/kWh in December 2024 to approximately $0.077/kWh in December 2025). Rising winter energy costs and cuts to the ERCOT (Texas Grid) led to a sharp increase in uneconomical mining hours between November and December. Regulatory action resumed in Xinjiang, China in December 2025 (inspections resulted in reduced mining operations, although no permanent loss of hashrate). Despite a short-term decline, the network increased by approximately 300 EH/s during 2025. As of this writing, the hashrate remains at the level of late 2025, at approximately 1,020 EH/s. While the recent decline in hashrate seems alarming, logarithmically, it is far less severe than the situation during China's mining ban in 2021. This represents a combination of cyclical and weather factors, rather than an ominous sign for the industry. The hashrate has since rebounded significantly, highlighting that many miners still consider it a viable economic activity. Based on our previously detailed segmented forecasting model, we now predict that computing power will reach 1.8 ZH/s by the end of 2026 and 2 ZH/s by the end of March 2027, one month later than previously predicted. [Image of a PNG file] Geographical Shift: The top three countries (the United States, China, and Russia) control approximately 68% of global computing power. The US market share increased by approximately 2 percentage points quarter-over-quarter. Driven by miners such as HIVE (300MW in Paraguay) and BTDR (40MW in Ethiopia), emerging markets such as Paraguay, Ethiopia, and Oman have entered the global top ten.

IV. Hash Price Dynamics

The hash price (a metric measuring miners' revenue per unit of computing power) peaked at approximately $63/PH/s/day in July and has been steadily declining in the fourth quarter of 2025.

By November, it had fallen to around $35-37/PH/s/day, a five-year low at the time. A brief rebound to $38-40 in late December and early January proved short-lived, with the hash price collapsing further in the first quarter of 2026, hitting around $28-30/PH/s/day in early March, a post-halving all-time low. This decline was driven by a combination of factors: record difficulty (reaching a peak of 155.97T after a 6.31% increase on October 29), a depressed Bitcoin price (about 31% lower than the October all-time high), and extremely low transaction fee revenue (consistently below 1% of the total block reward, with an average fee of approximately 0.018 BTC per block). This created the tightest profit environment since the April 2024 halving. Miners running mid-generation hardware (S19j Pro level, efficiency approximately 29.5 J/TH) with an average industrial electricity price of $0.05/kWh (S19 XP at $0.077/kWh) were already well below break-even by the end of the year, and the situation is expected to worsen further into 2026. Updated Forecast: The hash rate environment exceeded our previous expectations, briefly touching around $28/PH/s/day in late February before recovering to its current level of around $30-35. At these levels, miners running mid-generation hardware need to obtain less than 5 cents/kWh of electricity to maintain a profitable cash flow, while the latest generation units (below 15 J/TH) still retain considerable profit margins under typical industrial electricity prices. For the hash rate to sustainably recover to above $40, the Bitcoin price needs to challenge $100,000 by the end of the year, with the increase exceeding the continued growth in hash rate. Unless there is a substantial recovery in Bitcoin prices, we expect high-cost operators to capitulate further in the first half of 2026. The current mining economics are insufficient to incentivize a widespread hardware upgrade cycle. Hash prices may need to fall further first, leading to enough older-generation capacity and operators exiting the market, thus reducing network hashrate and difficulty. This could provide an entry point for new Bitcoin miners or incentivize upgrades to existing operations. However, despite continued squeeze on profit margins, network hashrate has remained unusually resilient. This is likely due to: state-backed mining with strategic rather than economically driven objectives, operators with extremely cheap or obsolete energy sources, and ASIC manufacturers tapping unsold inventory into their facilities to maintain order commitments from foundries like TSMC and Samsung. The mining sector's pain has led to miner capitulation, with many selling off their holdings. Listed mining companies have cumulatively reduced their treasury reserves by more than 15,000 BTC from peak levels. Core Scientific alone sold approximately 1,900 BTC (about $175 million) in January and plans to liquidate almost all of its remaining holdings in the first quarter of 2026; Bitdeer emptied its treasury in February; and Riot sold 1,818 BTC (about $162 million) in December 2025. We believe a Bitcoin price rebound to the $100,000 mark is not unrealistic. At that price, the hash rate is expected to recover to $37/PH/day. If the Bitcoin price remains below $80,000 for the remainder of the year, assuming the difficulty continues to rise, we predict the hash rate will continue to decline. In this scenario, hashrate may decline further as miners shut down unprofitable rigs, making it more likely that hash prices will plateau. If we see prices begin to challenge the all-time high of $126,000, we could see hash prices rise to $59/PH/day. The current hash price has fallen well below our forecast range, although we believe this is a temporary issue caused by the recent price drop and expect it to ease to the $30-40/PH/day range. The current hash price makes mining with various types of mining rigs unfeasible. At the current hash rate of $30/PH/day, any miner with an efficiency lower than the S19 XP and an electricity price of 6 cents/kWh or higher is losing money—we estimate this accounts for approximately 15-20% of the global mining fleet. V. Mining Cost Analysis 5.1 Overview The following table presents a breakdown of the unit Bitcoin cost for all covered miners in the fourth quarter of 2025. All figures are in USD/coin.

Key Observation:

  1. AI/HPC construction distorts the cost per coin metric for hybrid operators.

5.2 Company Details

MARA (MARA Holdings)

  • BTC Mining Amount: 2,011

  • Total Cost: $153,040/BTC

  • Cash Cost (excluding tax): $103,605/BTC MARA remains the largest publicly traded miner in terms of Q4 output. Its end-December hashrate was 53.2 EH/s. Electricity costs were $64,703/BTC, placing it in the middle range and reflecting its diverse geographic distribution and heavy reliance on third-party custody. Depreciation and amortization were the highest among its peers at $136,166/BTC, reflecting its large fleet. Total costs were significantly distorted by a $183.4 million income tax gain (driven by a fair value adjustment for BTC holdings). Excluding this non-profit income, the total cost rises to $240,407. It's worth noting that MARA officially authorized the sale of its entire 53,822 BTC reserve in its March 2026 financial report, marking a significant shift from its "HODL" strategy since July 2024. IREN (IREN Limited) BTC Mined: 1,664 Total Cost: $140,441/BTC Cash Cost: $58,462/BTC IREN has the lowest electricity cost per BTC ($34,325), thanks to favorable agreements and demand response revenue from its Childress facility in Texas. Its SBC is as high as $31,717/BTC, ranking second among its peers. IREN holds $3.7 billion in convertible bonds, the heaviest debt burden among its peers, but low coupon rates keep interest expenses manageable. CLSK (CleanSpark) BTC Mined: 1,821 Total Cost: $118,932/BTC Cash Cost (excluding tax): $71,188/BTC CleanSpark demonstrates exceptional operational discipline, with the lowest administrative and equity incentive costs among its peers. As a pure mining business (no hosting/HPC revenue), its analysis is straightforward. Extremely low interest costs ($830/BTC) reflect a low-leverage balance sheet.

RIOT (Riot Platforms)

  • BTC Mining Amount: 1,324

  • Total Cost: $170,366/BTC

  • Cash Cost (excluding tax): $102,538/BTC

    • Electricity cost was $49,196/BTC, benefiting from a $9.9 million grid demand response credit. Its strategic core is the Corsicana site, of which 600MW has been allocated to AI workloads, making it one of the largest single-site operators in North America.

RIOT (Riot Platforms)

  • BTC Mining Amount: 1,324

  • Total Cost: $170,366/BTC

  • Cash Cost (excluding tax): $102,538/BTC

    • Electricity cost was $49,196/BTC, benefiting from a $9.9 million grid demand response credit.

At the heart of its strategy ...

CORZ (Core Scientific)

  • BTC Mining Quantity: 421

  • Total Cost: $168,693/BTC

  • Cash Cost: $110,282/BTC Q4 was a milestone for CORZ's transformation towards AI, with custody revenue accounting for 39% of total revenue. VI. Mining Stock Performance and Valuation

The valuation premium for AI/HPC continued to widen in the fourth quarter. Miners with signed HPC contracts are currently trading at 12.3x EV/NTM Sales, while pure mining companies are trading at only 5.9x.

The industry has fundamentally differentiated into: "Infrastructure Companies": WULF, CORZ, CIFR, HUT "Mining Companies": MARA, CLSK, RIOT, HIVE Whether AI-driven high valuations are justified depends on execution: not all announced agreements translate into operational infrastructure, and capital requirements remain enormous.

VII. First Quarter of 2026 and Future Outlook

  1. Hash price recovery depends on Bitcoin price: At a Bitcoin price of $70,000 and a hash price of $30, many mid-generation fleets are on the verge of losses. If Bitcoin falls below $70,000, it could trigger a large-scale capitulation, which in turn would benefit the survivors.

  2. Next-generation hardware deployment: Bitmain's S23 series and SEALMINER A3 (both with efficiencies below 10 J/TH) will be deployed at scale in the first half of 2026, accelerating the replacement of older models.
